planting.note: Propagate from stem cuttings taken in spring or summer; lay cane sections horizontally on moist potting mix for easy rooting. Dieffenbachia is frost-tender but can grow outdoors year-round in zone 9b–10 in a sheltered, shaded spot. Bring container plants indoors when temperatures drop below 50°F.

directions: Grow in bright indirect light to moderate shade in well-draining, humus-rich potting mix or garden soil; dieffenbachia tolerates low light better than most large-leafed plants but grows most vigorously with filtered morning light. The bold, patterned leaves in cream, white, and green are among the most dramatic of all shade-tolerant foliage plants. Allow the top inch of soil to dry between waterings and mist foliage in dry conditions; avoid cold drafts and temperatures below 55°F. All parts contain calcium oxalate raphides — contact with the sap causes intense mouth and throat irritation, and the plant should be kept away from children and pets.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Dieffenbachia
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Dieffenbachia seguine
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Indoor Foliage Plants

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, frost-free location or in containers moved indoors during cold spells. In zone 9 grow in a protected, deep-shade spot or use as a container plant on a shaded porch. The broad, undivided fronds are unlike typical fern foliage and provide a bold, tropical texture in shade gardens.

directions: Grow in bright to moderate shade in well-draining, humus-rich soil or a loose bark-based potting mix; bird nest fern grows as an epiphyte in nature, perching on tree trunks with its roots in accumulated leaf litter. The glossy, strap-like fronds emerge from a central rosette and can reach 4 feet long in ideal conditions. Avoid pouring water into the central cup where new fronds emerge, as this encourages rot; water the root zone and allow the mix to approach dryness between waterings. Fertilize monthly during the growing season with a dilute balanced fertilizer. This fern is excellent in bathrooms and covered patios where humidity is naturally higher.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ferns for the Landscape
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Asplenium nidus
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Asplenium nidus

planting.note: Propagate from stem cuttings, which root easily in moist potting mix; pepino does not breed true from seed. Plant after frost risk passes in full sun. In zone 9 it can be grown as a perennial subshrub, often persisting for several seasons if protected from the rare hard freeze. A single plant can produce dozens of fruits.

directions: Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil; pepino melon is a relative of tomato and eggplant that produces cream to pale-purple-striped fruits shaped like a pear or egg, with flesh tasting of a mild blend of melon and cucumber. Grow on a stake or trellis to keep fruit off the ground and improve air circulation. The plant benefits from regular fertilization with a tomato-type fertilizer and consistent watering; fruit quality declines with drought stress. Harvest when fruits turn golden yellow with purple stripes and yield slightly to pressure — they are eaten fresh, in fruit salads, or as a savory side dish in Andean cuisine where they originate.
sources: California Rare Fruit Growers — Pepino Fact Sheet
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Unusual Vegetables and Fruits

planting.note: Propagate only from stem cuttings — African Blue Basil is a sterile hybrid that produces no viable seed. Plant after last frost in full sun; in zone 9 it grows as a semi-woody perennial, often surviving several seasons if the winter is mild. It is the most ornamental and longest-lived of all basils.

directions: Grow in well-drained soil in full sun; African Blue Basil is a sterile interspecific hybrid of Ocimum kilimandscharicum and O. basilicum that combines the camphor-basil heat tolerance of its African parent with the culinary basil flavor of its Italian parent. The plants produce attractive purple flowers continuously without the bolting problem common to culinary basils — but because they are sterile, the flowers attract rather than deplete the plant, making it one of the most bee-attractive herbs in the summer garden. The leaves have a pronounced camphor-basil aroma alongside traditional basil notes and are used in cooking wherever strong basil flavor is desired. It grows robustly as a subshrub in zone 9 and can be lightly pruned to maintain shape.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Basil in Florida
Herb Society of America — Ocimum species

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs or rooted cuttings in spring. In zone 8b–9 Turk's cap dies to the ground in hard freezes but reliably resprouts from the root; established plants can regrow 6–8 feet by summer. In zone 10 it remains evergreen. Tolerates extreme heat and part-shade — ideal for difficult spots under trees.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in virtually any well-drained soil — Turk's cap tolerates poor clay, rocky soil, drought, deep shade, and heat once established, making it one of the most adaptable native shrubs for the Gulf Coast and South Texas. The twisted, never-fully-opening scarlet flowers, held above the foliage from summer through frost, are shaped like a Turkish fez and are a magnet for hummingbirds, butterflies, and bees. The small red fruits that follow are edible — sweet, slightly bland, and used in jams or eaten fresh as a wilderness snack. Hard-prune to the ground in late winter to encourage vigorous, tidy regrowth; do not fertilize regularly as excess fertility promotes leggy growth.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Turk's Cap
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Malvaviscus arboreus
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Native Plants for Florida Landscapes
